# Sampling with minimizers {#sec-intro-minimizers}

The sketching methods presented in @sec-intro-sketching compress a sequence into a compact summary, but they treat the sequence as a simple "bag" of /kmers: two sequences with a high Jaccard index are considered similar regardless of *where* the shared /kmers appear.

Many bioinformatics tasks, such as read alignment or index construction, require something stronger: a guarantee that two sequences sharing a long local exact match will also share at least one selected /kmer at corresponding positions.
This *locality* property is what distinguishes minimizers from general sketching, and is the subject of this chapter.

## Definition and fundamental properties {#sec-minimizer-definition}

The concept of minimizers was introduced independently by two groups in 2003--2004.
@schleimer2003winnowing introduced *winnowing* in the context of document fingerprinting and plagiarism detection: a sliding window scans the /kmers of a document and the smallest /kmer (according to some ordering) is selected as a fingerprint.
@roberts2004reducing proposed the same idea for biological sequences, introducing the term *minimizer*, with the explicit goal of providing a locality-preserving sketch: any two sequences sharing a long exact match are guaranteed to share at least one minimizer.

A minimizer scheme is parametrized by three values: the /kmer length $k$, the window size $w$, and a total ordering $\prec$ on /kmers.
A *window* of size $w$ is a set of $w$ consecutive /kmers, spanning a substring of length $\ell = w + k - 1$.
The *minimizer* of a window is the /kmer that is smallest under $\prec$, with ties broken by selecting the leftmost occurrence^[Choosing the leftmost occurrence is a convention, some schemes use the rightmost one or select all tied /kmers.].
Sliding the window one position at a time along the sequence yields the *minimizer sketch* of that sequence: the (deduplicated) sequence of minimizer positions.

:::{.callout-caution}
## Different notations

Depending on the context, multiple notations are used in the literature.
The parameters that we defined above as $k$ and $\ell$ are sometimes denoted $m$ and $k$ respectively, meaning that one minimizer of size $m$ is selected from each substring of size $k = w + m - 1$.
:::

### Window guarantee

An essential property of minimizers is the so-called *window guarantee*.

::: {#pty-window-guarantee}
## Window guarantee

Any window of *w* consecutive /kmers, i.e. any substring of length $\ell = w + k - 1$, contains at least one minimizer.
:::

This property has two important consequences that make minimizers especially useful in practice.

::: {#pty-conservation}
## Conservation

Any pair of sequences sharing an exact match of length at least $\ell = w + k - 1$ will share at least one minimizer.
:::

::: {#pty-bounded-dist}
## Bounded distance

The distance between two consecutive minimizers is at most $w$.
:::

These two properties together ensure that minimizers can serve as *anchors* for sequence comparison: shared minimizers between a query and a reference indicate candidate alignment positions, and the bounded distance guarantees that no long match is missed [@minimizers-review].

### Density

The *density* of a minimizer scheme is the expected fraction of /kmers selected over all positions of a random sequence.
Since consecutive minimizers are at most $w$ positions apart (@pty-bounded-dist), the density is always lower-bounded by $1/w$.

## Orderings {#sec-minimizer-orderings}

### Lexicographic ordering

### Random ordering

## Super-/kmers and sequence partitioning {#sec-skmers}

## Canonical minimizers {#sec-canonical-minimizers-intro}

## Improving conservation {#sec-minimizer-conservation}

### Syncmers

### Strobemers

## Practical applications {#sec-minimizer-applications}

### Read alignment

### Sequence classification and metagenomics

### Partitioning indexes

### Genome assembly

---

TBA

- historical context [@schleimer2003winnowing;@roberts2004reducing]
- random vs lexicographic
- practical use cases [@minimizers-review]
- improving conservation [@syncmers;@shaw-conservation;@strobemers;@strobealign]
- low density schemes detailed in @sec-low-density-minimizers
